section.title: The Woman in the Basket
section.sectionRationale: The seventh night vision completes the cleansing begun by the flying scroll. Wickedness itself, personified as a woman, is sealed inside a measuring basket under a lead weight and carried off to Shinar, the land of Babylon, to be housed there. Sin is not merely punished in individuals; it is removed bodily from the land and sent back to its home.
section.thesis: God does not only judge sinners under the curse; He removes the very principle of wickedness from among His people, sealing it up and deporting it to Babylon, so that the restored land may be holy for His dwelling.
section.frameNote: One movement: the measuring basket brought forward, Wickedness revealed and shut inside under a lead cover, and the two winged women carrying the basket to build it a house in the land of Shinar.

verses.text: Then the angel who was speaking with me came forward and told me, “Now lift up your eyes and see what is approaching.”
“What is it?” I asked. And he replied, “A measuring {{ephah|basket}} is going forth.” Then he continued, “This is their iniquity in all the land.”
And behold, the cover of lead was raised, and there was a woman sitting inside the basket.
“This is {{rishah|Wickedness}},” he said. And he shoved her down into the basket, pushing down the lead cover over its opening.
Then I lifted up my eyes and saw two women approaching, with the wind in their wings. Their wings were like those of a stork, and they lifted up the basket between heaven and earth.
“Where are they taking the basket?” I asked the angel who was speaking with me.
“To build a house for it in the land of {{shinar|Shinar}},” he told me. “And when it is ready, the basket will be set there on its pedestal.”

ephah.gloss: A dry measure of about twenty two liters, a standard commercial container used in the marketplace. Its appearance here, large enough to hold a seated woman, links wickedness to dishonest trade, the false measures and cheating in the market that the prophets so often condemned. The basket becomes the vessel in which sin is measured, sealed, and shipped away.

rishah.gloss: Wickedness, guilt, the settled condition of being in the wrong before God. The angel personifies it as a woman inside the basket, showing that God deals not only with individual sinful acts but with the very principle and power of evil at work among His people, which He removes bodily from the land.

shinar.gloss: Shinar, the ancient name for the plain of Babylonia, the site of Babel and its tower, the archetypal land of rebellion against God. Sending wickedness there to be given a house on a pedestal means returning evil to its spiritual home, where it will be enshrined and idolized, far from the cleansed land of God's people.

basic.context: This is the seventh night vision, and it pairs with the sixth. Where the flying scroll judged individual sinners, this vision removes wickedness itself. A commercial basket, an ephah, comes forward. Inside sits a woman the angel names Wickedness. He shoves her back down and seals the basket with a heavy lead lid. Then two winged women carry the basket off to Shinar, that is Babylon, to build it a house there. Sin is measured, sealed, and deported to its true home.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a symbolic vision, and its images are meant to be read together. The basket is a marketplace measure, tying wickedness to dishonest trade. The woman personifies evil as a power, not just a deed. The lead cover shows it firmly contained. The journey to Shinar shows sin returned to Babylon, the biblical home of rebellion, where it belongs. The point is a thorough, decisive cleansing of the land.
basic.originalLanguages: The container is an ephah, a standard dry measure from the market, hinting at the cheating and false weights the prophets condemned. The figure inside is rishah, wickedness itself personified. And the destination is Shinar, the plain of Babel and Babylon, the archetypal land of rebellion against God, where evil is given a house and a pedestal, enshrined at its own source.
basic.typology: The removal of wickedness from the land points forward to the final, complete removal of sin from God's people and God's world. Babylon as the home of evil anticipates the Babylon of Revelation, the great harlot city of rebellion, finally judged and thrown down so that the holy city can come.
basic.christ: This vision shows God's determination to take sin away, not merely to cover it over. That purpose is accomplished in Christ, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, who removes our transgressions as far as the east is from the west. What the vision pictures as sealing evil in a basket and shipping it to Babylon, the gospel accomplishes at the cross and will complete when Babylon falls forever.
basic.god: God is holy and thorough. He does not tolerate a little wickedness among His people; He measures it, seals it, and sends it away to its own place. He is sovereign even over evil, appointing where it goes and confining it under a weight it cannot lift. And He is committed to a clean dwelling, a land and a people fit for His holy presence.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 17 and 18 show Babylon as the home of wickedness, finally judged. Micah 6 condemns the false measures of the marketplace. 1 John 3 says Christ appeared to take away sins. Psalm 103 says God removes our transgressions from us.
basic.oneStory: The cleansing of the land continues: wickedness itself is removed, sealed up, and sent home to Babylon. This anticipates the day when sin is taken away entirely and Babylon falls forever, so that the holy city and its cleansed people can dwell with God. The whole story bends toward a world with the wickedness finally deported and destroyed.

context.literary: The seventh of the eight night visions, forming a pair with the sixth. Together they cleanse the covenant community, the scroll dealing with guilty individuals and the basket with the principle of wickedness itself, both preparing the land for the return of God's presence promised in the visions of the priest and the governor.
context.historical: The returned exiles had themselves come out of Babylon. To see wickedness carried back to Shinar would strike home: the sin that had sent them into exile is now sent away from them, back to the land of their captivity. The market basket also spoke to a community tempted by dishonest trade in hard economic times.
context.canonical: Shinar reaches back to Genesis 10 and 11, the plain of Babel and the tower of rebellion, and forward to Revelation, where Babylon is the great harlot, the seat of the world's wickedness, finally judged. The vision sits on this long biblical arc from Babel to Babylon's fall.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure the restored community that God will remove wickedness itself from the land, not merely punish offenders, sealing evil up and returning it to Babylon, its spiritual home, so that the land may be holy and fit for God's dwelling.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes God's decisive removal of wickedness; it is not a set of instructions for us. The enduring truths are that God takes sin away rather than tolerating it, that He is sovereign over evil, and that He is preparing a clean dwelling for Himself.

debates.question: Why is wickedness pictured as a woman in a basket?

views.label: A neutral personification for the Hebrew noun
views.case: Wickedness, rishah, is a feminine noun, so the vision naturally personifies it as a woman, with no comment on women themselves; the image simply gives evil a visible form to be contained and removed.

views.label: An echo of the seductive harlot figure
views.case: The image anticipates the harlot of Babylon in Revelation, wickedness as a seductive, idolatrous power enshrined in a false temple, tying market corruption to spiritual adultery.

debates.bearing: Either way the vision personifies evil as a power to be removed, and connects it to Babylon, the enduring biblical symbol of organized rebellion against God.

debates.question: What does building a house for the basket in Shinar mean?

views.label: Enshrining evil in its own place
views.case: A house on a pedestal is temple language; wickedness is given its own false temple in Babylon, worshipped at its source, decisively separated from God's cleansed land.

views.label: Returning sin to where it came from
views.case: Since the exiles came out of Babylon, sending wickedness back to Shinar dramatizes that the evil which caused the exile is now expelled from the restored community.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that wickedness is firmly and finally removed from God's people and confined to Babylon, awaiting its own final judgment.

study.originalLanguages: The vision turns on three loaded words. The container is an ephah, an ordinary marketplace measure, which quietly ties wickedness to dishonest trade, the rigged scales and false weights the prophets hated. Seated inside is rishah, wickedness personified, not a single sin but the settled power of evil, so heavy it must be forced down and pinned under a lead lid. And the basket is flown to Shinar, the plain of Babel and Babylon, the Bible's standing name for organized rebellion against God, where evil is given a house on a pedestal, a false temple in which it is enshrined at its own source. The whole scene says that God removes wickedness bodily from His land and returns it to its home to await its doom.

typology.type: Wickedness sealed up and carried away
typology.fulfillment: Christ taking away the sin of the world and God removing our transgressions from us, John 1:29 and Psalm 103:12.

typology.type: Babylon as the house of wickedness
typology.fulfillment: The great harlot Babylon of Revelation, the seat of the world's rebellion, finally judged and fallen, Revelation 18:2.

typology.type: The cleansed land fit for God
typology.fulfillment: The holy city, the new Jerusalem, into which nothing unclean will ever enter, Revelation 21:27.

study.christ: This vision presses one great truth: God does not merely manage sin, He takes it away. He measures wickedness, seals it under a weight it cannot lift, and deports it to Babylon. That determination is fulfilled in Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. On the cross He did not simply cover our guilt; He bore it away, so that God could remove our transgressions from us as far as the east is from the west. And what the vision shows in miniature, evil confined to Babylon and enshrined there, the gospel completes in the fall of Babylon the great, when the whole seat of rebellion is thrown down forever and every trace of wickedness is removed from the world God is cleansing for His dwelling. The vision that sends sin away finds its yes and amen in the Savior who carried it away.
study.god: The God of this vision is relentlessly holy and utterly thorough. He is not content to punish a few offenders while wickedness itself keeps working among His people; He roots out the principle of evil, measures it, seals it, and sends it away. He is sovereign even over wickedness, appointing its container, its weight, and its destination, so that evil goes exactly where He sends it and no further. And behind the whole scene is His settled purpose to have a clean land and a holy people, a dwelling fit for His presence, which is why He will not rest until wickedness is removed to its own place and finally destroyed.

crossReferences.note: Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, the fulfillment of wickedness being carried off.
Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, the final judgment of the house of wickedness in Shinar.
As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions, echoing the deportation of sin.
The plain of Shinar and the tower of Babel, the origin of Babylon as the land of rebellion.

study.oneStory: The seventh vision completes the cleansing: wickedness itself is measured, sealed, and flown back to Babylon, its home. This carries forward the Bible's long war on evil, from Babel to the fall of Babylon the great, and points to Christ who takes away the sin of the world. The story runs on toward the holy city into which nothing unclean will ever come, a cleansed people at home with a holy God.

quiz.q: What does the woman in the basket represent?

options.text: Wickedness itself, personified and removed from the land.
options.note: The angel names her This is Wickedness.

options.text: A particular sinful woman being judged.
options.note: She personifies wickedness as a power, not one individual.

options.text: The nation of Israel going into exile.
options.note: The vision removes wickedness, not the people, from the land.

options.text: A goddess the people should worship.
options.note: She is evil to be expelled, not a deity to be honored.

quiz.q: Where is the basket carried, and why?

options.text: To Shinar, that is Babylon, the home of rebellion, to be enshrined there.
options.note: Wickedness is returned to Babylon, its spiritual home, and given a house.

options.text: To Jerusalem, to be kept in the temple.
options.note: The opposite: it is removed far from the holy land, not kept in it.

options.text: To Egypt, to be destroyed at once.
options.note: It goes to Shinar, and is housed rather than immediately destroyed.

options.text: Nowhere; the basket stays in the land.
options.note: The whole point is that wickedness is carried away from the land.

quiz.q: How does this vision relate to the flying scroll before it?

options.text: Together they cleanse the land, one judging sinners, the other removing wickedness itself.
options.note: The pair prepares the land for God's holy presence.

options.text: They contradict each other.
options.note: They complement each other as a matched pair of cleansing visions.

options.text: They have nothing to do with each other.
options.note: They are deliberately paired to complete the theme of cleansing.

options.text: Both are about rebuilding the temple walls.
options.note: Both are about moral cleansing, not construction.

quiz.q: What does the marketplace basket suggest about the wickedness in view?

options.text: It is tied to dishonest trade and false measures.
options.note: The ephah is a commercial measure, linking wickedness to cheating in the market.

options.text: It is only about religious ritual.
options.note: The container points specifically to commerce, though sin is broader.

options.text: It has nothing to do with everyday life.
options.note: The market basket roots wickedness in ordinary economic dealings.

options.text: It shows the people were too poor to sin.
options.note: The basket exposes sin in trade, not poverty as innocence.

quiz.q: How does the vision point to Christ and the gospel?

options.text: God's purpose to take sin away is fulfilled in Christ, who bears it away for His people.
options.note: The Lamb of God takes away the sin of the world, John 1:29.

options.text: It shows sin can never really be removed.
options.note: The opposite: it shows God decisively removing wickedness.

options.text: It teaches we must carry our own sin to Babylon.
options.note: God removes the sin; we cannot deport our own guilt.

options.text: It has no gospel meaning.
options.note: It anticipates Christ taking away the sin of the world.

questions: God removes wickedness rather than tolerating it among His people. Where are you tempted to manage a sin rather than let God take it away?
The basket tied wickedness to the marketplace. How honest are you in your work, your money, and your dealings when no one is watching?
Wickedness was sent back to Babylon, its true home. What sinful pattern in your life needs to be sent away rather than given a house?
Christ takes away the sin of the world. How does it comfort you that God bears your guilt away rather than merely covering it over?
God is preparing a clean land for His presence. What would it look like this week to cooperate with His cleansing work in you?

translationNotes.note: Measuring basket renders ephah, a dry measure of about twenty two liters; a footnote in many editions notes the literal An ephah, tying the image to commerce and false measures.

This is their iniquity follows the reading of one Hebrew manuscript, the Septuagint, and Syriac; most Hebrew manuscripts read their appearance or literally their eye, a minor textual variant that does not change the sense of judgment on sin.
Shinar is the ancient name for Babylonia, the plain of Babel; building a house on a pedestal uses temple language, picturing wickedness enshrined in a false sanctuary at its own home.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 5:5-11. All 7 verses reproduced in order. No em or en dashes present in this passage.
